Utilities Up, But not by Much, on Cyclical Bias - Utilities Roundup
data.description:
Shares of power producers rose, but not by as much as the broad market, as traders rotated into more cyclical areas of the stock market.

The SPDR Select Sector Utilities exchange-traded fund, which tracks the utilities industry group of the S&P 500, rose 0.4%, but remains one of the leaders among the 11 S&P 500 sectors for the year to date.

data.description:
Communications-services rose as traders rotated back into risky sectors, betting that the Iran war will not derail a U.S. economic expansion.

"CBS Mornings" co-host Gayle King signed a new deal with CBS News, securing a high-profile network talent during a dramatic shake-up of the news organization.

Tech Up on Stronger-Than-Anticipated Data - Tech Roundup
data.description:
Shares of technology companies rose after stronger-than-anticipated economic data.

Memory chip makers such as Sandisk, Seagate and Micron rallied.

The economically sensitive chip makers were among the biggest losers in the tech sector during the height of Iran war worries.

Apple's pricing strategy on its newest devices suggested it wanted to take advantage of competitors' struggles with memory-chip prices and win market share by selling with lean margins.

Cloud-storage concern Box rallied after it posted better-than-anticipated earnings growth.

Software maker GitLab fell after it warned of slowing sales this year.

CoreWeave rallied after the cloud-computing giant unveiled a multiyear partnership with AI firm Perplexity.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 1739ET

data.title: Financials Up as Private-Credit, Cryptocurrency Firms Rebound - Financials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of banks and other financial institutions rose as private-credit and bitcoin worries abated.

Cryptocurrency firms such as Coinbase Global and Robinhood Markets rallied alongside bitcoin, which shot up roughly 8% to multiweek highs above $73,000.

Among private-credit firms, shares of Blackstone rose 2.9%. Blue Owl rose slightly.

Governments in developed nations are set to sell a record amount of bonds this year in an increasingly risky environment, while a small number of companies plan to borrow heavily to fund building AI capacity, the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development said.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 1738ET

data.title: Consumer Cos Up on Mixed Earnings, Strong Economic Data - Consumer Roundup
data.description:
Consumer companies rose after mixed earnings reports and strong economic data.

Abercrombie & Fitch shares fell after the youth retailer's notched higher sales in its fiscal fourth quarter and guided for growth to continue over the coming year.

Shares of sneaker maker Adidas dropped after the German company issued operating profit projection for 2026 short of Wall Street expectations, partly due to tariffs.

Bath & Body Works reported lower fourth-quarter profit, but said its strategy pivot to refocus on its core products was making progress.

Jack Daniel's maker Brown-Forman said sales in its developed markets were continuing to decline due to a number of macroeconomic pressures, some of which could persist.

Similarly, Italian distiller Davide Campari-Milano warned it expects 2026 sales and earnings growth to be tempered by trade tariffs and currency effects.

The Institute for Supply Management's purchasing managers index for services providers rose to 56.1 in February from 53.8 in January.

Private employers added more workers than anticipated in February.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 1738ET

Health Care Up, But not by Much, on Cyclical Bias - Health Care Roundup
data.description:
Health-care companies rose, but not by as much as the broad market, as traders rotated into cyclical sectors.

Bayer posted a steeper net loss for the fourth quarter, as it booked litigation charges as part of its yearslong court battles over its Roundup weedkiller. Bayer said growth of its Kerendia drug for kidney disease would partially offset the loss of patent protection of blood thinner Xarelto and eye medicine Eylea.

Vaccine maker Moderna agreed to pay $950 million to settle patent litigation by Arbutus Biopharma and Genevant Sciences GmbH.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 1736ET

data.title: Industrials Up After Jobs Data - Industrials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of industrial and transportation companies rose after surprisingly strong private-jobs market data.

U.S. private employers added 63,000 jobs last month, versus the revised 11,000 seen in ADP's January data, according to the payroll firm.

The Federal Reserve's Beige Book regional economic survey suggested the labor-market is firming up in most districts, but most businesses surveyed indicated the outlook was cloudy.

There were mixed signals about electric-car demand. Ford Motor said EV sales. Volvo Car is preparing to increase production of its fully electric EX60 sports-utility vehicle to meet strong demand from key countries such as Sweden and Germany.

Container shipping lines and defense contractors ticked down but retained the bulk of their recent gains as war continued to rage throughout the Middle East.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 1734ET

data.title: Noem defends $220 million ad campaign amid lawmaker scrutiny
data.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem on Wednesday defended the government's spending of $220 million on an advertising campaign for border security amid questions from lawmakers about the contracting process and Republican-connected firms awarded the money.

During a hearing before a U.S. House of Representatives panel, Representative Joe Neguse, a Democrat from Colorado, grilled Noem over a contracting notice that said the Department of Homeland Security had limited competition to four companies in February 2025, citing an urgent need to stop illegal immigration.

"It is fraud, and ultimately, I think there's going to be accountability," Neguse said.

The criticism came after Noem - a top official overseeing President Donald Trump's aggressive immigration crackdown - was questioned by both Democrats and Republicans before a Senate panel on Wednesday about the contract and process to select the companies. 

The ads produced with the funds prominently featured Noem, including in a scene filmed on horseback at Mount Rushmore in the former South Dakota governor's home state.

Noem said on Tuesday that the contract was awarded through "a competitive process" and that no political appointees were involved. On Wednesday, she said the contract was "all done correctly, all done legally."

A DHS spokesperson said on Tuesday that career government employees oversee the procurement process.

The contract was awarded to two companies linked to long-time Republican operatives, records show, details first reported by the Associated Press and ProPublica.

One of the companies, Safe America Media, was incorporated in Delaware a week before winning the contract. The company was awarded $143 million, federal records show.

The second, the Louisiana-based firm People Who Think, was awarded $77 million.

Neither company could be immediately reached for comment.

The Strategy Group, a company run by the husband of outgoing DHS spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin, said on Wednesday that it was paid $226,000 as a subcontractor, a small portion of the total.

(Reporting by Ted Hesson in Washington; Editing by Matthew Lewis)

By Ted Hesson

data.title: Materials Up as Dollar Retreats - Materials Roundup
data.description:
Producers of metals, grains and other raw materials rose slightly as the dollar retreated against rivals buoying metals prices.

Copper futures rose 1.4% to $5.86 a pound. Gold and silver futures, traditional safe havens in times of war and geopolitical uncertainty, finished mixed.

A speculative run-up ahead of the Iran war has prompted a rotation into alternative safe havens.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 1732ET

data.title: Energy Down as Oil Rally Slows - Energy Roundup
data.description:
Shares of energy companies fell as a surge in oil prices slowed. Oil ticked up to $74.66 a barrel in New York as war continued to rage throughout the Middle East.

The Strait of Hormuz, a key conduit for global energy markets, has all but closed to tankers.

Goldman Sachs boosted its projections for second-quarter oil prices by about $10 a barrel, pegging globally traded Brent oil at $76 a barrel.

Similarly, exports of liquefied natural gas from the Middle East are likely to fall sharply, due to Iranian attacks on infrastructure and other war impacts.

President Trump's promise to protect the Strait of Hormuz with naval escorts and provide government-backed marine insurance underscores the urgent need to restore flows of energy from the Middle East before soaring prices rip through the world economy.

The SPDR Select Sector Energy exchange-traded fund, which tracks the energy sector of the S&P 500, fell Wednesday, but remains up about 23% for the year to date -- by far the biggest gainer of the 11 S&P 500 sectors.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 1731ET

ICE Canada Weekly Outlook : U.S. Biodiesel Mandate to Be Boon for Canola
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Canola is poised for more gains as the trade awaits the Trump administration's decision on the latest biodiesel mandate, said Jamie Wilton, broker with RJ O'Brien in Winnipeg.

Wilton said the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency sent its recommendations and a decision on them has yet to come from the White House. He said it's unclear at the moment how Canadian canola could play into that biodiesel mandate. If it's included, then it's a matter of volume and how much its tax credit will be.

The EPA proposed biomass-based diesel at 5.61 billion gallons, a jump of 67.5% from 2025 levels.

"It's a pile of demand that's coming down the line," Wilton said, noting that Chicago soyoil has already been on the rise for some time.

Even if the Trump administration excluded Canadian canola, Wilton stressed there will still be an upside for it.

"Burning beanoil in the fuel tank, canola is going to pick up demand on the food side," he said.

Wilton said the impact from the White House on U.S. soybeans could mirror that of corn when ethanol production was increased.

"You're ramping up biodiesel production, so you will need the feedstocks to do that," he said.

As for the Statistics Canada forecast on planted area in 2026/27, Wilton suggested the report likely won't have much of an impact on the canola market the day it's released.

The trade widely expects planted canola acres to increase from 21.62 million seeded in 2025/26. Some in the market pegged the number at 22.50 million acres, cautioning yields are likely to drop to normal levels in 2026/27.

"We'll need every stitch of canola we can produce," Wilton said.

He added, the Canadian Grain Commission should be soon showing increases in canola exports in its weekly reports. The destinations will be confirmed in the CGC's monthly reports, with the trade looking for a major increase of canola to China.

In the meantime, with the latest Middle East war, Wilton said it's difficult to see where the commodities will go "from minute to minute and hour by hour."


Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 1701ET
